::: proof

Adapted from [@klein2003survival, §8.3, Theoretical Note 1, p. 257].

Assume that the censoring is noninformative
(conditional on $\vx$, censoring times are independent of event times)
and that there are no tied event times,
so each ordered event time $t_i$ corresponds to a unique subject.
Let $R(t_i)$ be the risk set at $t_i$ —
$\{j : \tilde T_j \ge t_i\}$ per the [risk set definition](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#def-risk-set).

Fix any candidate subject $f \in R(t_i)$ and condition on the event
"exactly one failure occurs at $t_i$, among subjects in $R(t_i)$".
The conditional probability that this failure is subject $f$ (as
opposed to some other subject in $R(t_i)$) follows from the
definition of conditional probability,
$\Pr(A \mid B \cap C) = \Pr(A \cap B \mid C) / \Pr(B \mid C)$,
applied with

- $A$ = "subject $f$ fails at $t_i$",
- $B$ = "exactly one failure occurs at $t_i$",
- $C$ = "the risk set at $t_i$ is $R(t_i)$".

Under the no-ties assumption $A \subset B$ (subject $f$ failing at
$t_i$ is automatically a one-failure event), so $A \cap B = A$ and
$\Pr(A \cap B \mid C) = \Pr(A \mid C)$. The ratio simplifies to

$$
\ba
\Pr\sb{f \text{ fails} \mid 1 \text{ failure at } t_i,\ R(t_i)}
&= \frac{\Pr\sb{f \text{ fails at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}}
         {\Pr\sb{1 \text{ failure at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}}.
\ea
$$

Since $j \in R(t_i)$ means $\tilde{T}_j = \min(T_j,\,C_j) \ge t_i$
(see the [risk set definition](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#def-risk-set)),
we have both $T_j \ge t_i$ and $C_j \ge t_i$.
Under noninformative censoring ($T_j$ and $C_j$ are conditionally
independent given $\vx_j$), the event $C_j \ge t_i$ carries no
additional information about the distribution of $T_j$ given $\vx_j$,
so conditioning on $j \in R(t_i)$ is equivalent to conditioning on
$T_j \ge t_i$ alone.
By the [hazard definition](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#def-hazard),
$\haz(t \mid \vx)$ is the instantaneous
conditional failure rate at time $t$, given that the subject has
survived to at least $t$; for a small interval of width $\Delta t$:

$$
\Pr\sb{T_j \in [t_i,\, t_i + \Delta t) \mid j \in R(t_i)}
= \Pr\sb{T_j \in [t_i,\, t_i + \Delta t) \mid T_j \ge t_i}
\;\approx\; \haz(t_i \mid \vx_j)\,\Delta t.
$$

Apply this to the candidate $f$ (and, more generally, to any
$j \in R(t_i)$, since we'll need the same expression for every
subject when we form the partial-likelihood denominator).
Viewed as a function of $j \in R(t_i)$ for fixed $t_i$,
the right-hand side $\haz(t_i \mid \vx_j)\,\Delta t$ is proportional
to $\haz(t_i \mid \vx_j)$, because $\Delta t$ is the same constant
for every subject in the risk set.
By @thm-ph-haz-decomp,
$\haz(t_i \mid \vx_j) = \haz_0(t_i)\,\theta(\vx_j)$,
and $\haz_0(t_i)$ is likewise a constant shared by all subjects at $t_i$, so

$$
\ba
\Pr\sb{j \text{ fails at } t_i \mid j \in R(t_i)}
\;&\approx\; \haz(t_i \mid \vx_j)\,\Delta t
\\
\;&\propto\; \haz(t_i \mid \vx_j)
&& \text{(as a function of } j \in R(t_i)\text{, fixed } t_i\text{)}
\\
&= \haz_0(t_i)\,\theta(\vx_j).
\ea
$$

Treating subjects' individual failure events as conditionally
independent given $R(t_i)$ (under the Cox model assumption that,
conditional on covariates, each subject's failure time is
independent of all other subjects' failure times;
see [@klein2003survival, §8.3]),
the probability of exactly one failure in $[t_i, t_i + \Delta t)$
is, by inclusion-exclusion,

$$
\Pr\sb{1 \text{ failure at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}
= \sum_{k \in R(t_i)}
    \Pr\sb{k \text{ fails} \mid R(t_i)}
    \prod_{m \in R(t_i),\, m \ne k} \Pr\sb{m \text{ survives} \mid R(t_i)}.
$$

By the same hazard approximation,
$\Pr\sb{m \text{ fails} \mid R(t_i)} \approx \haz_0(t_i)\,\theta(\vx_m)\,\Delta t = O(\Delta t)$,
so each product $\prod_{m \ne k} \Pr\sb{m \text{ survives} \mid R(t_i)} = \prod_{m \ne k}(1 - O(\Delta t)) = 1 - O(\Delta t)$,
and dropping $O(\Delta t^2)$ cross-terms (each pair of co-failures has
probability $O(\Delta t^2)$) gives

$$
\Pr\sb{1 \text{ failure at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}
\;\approx\;
\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \haz_0(t_i)\,\theta(\vx_k)\,\Delta t.
$$

Substituting both into the conditional probability
and canceling the common factor $\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t$:

$$
\ba
\Pr\sb{f \text{ fails} \mid 1 \text{ failure at } t_i,\ R(t_i)}
&\approx \frac{\haz_0(t_i)\,\theta(\vx_f)\,\Delta t}
         {\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \haz_0(t_i)\,\theta(\vx_k)\,\Delta t}
\\
&= \frac{\theta(\vx_f)}{\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \theta(\vx_k)}.
\ea
$$

The first display step uses $\approx$ because it inherits the
first-order hazard approximation $\Pr\{T_j \in [t_i, t_i + \Delta t)
\mid T_j \ge t_i\} \approx \haz(t_i \mid \vx_j)\,\Delta t$ (the
*conditional* probability — the hazard's defining quantity, given
the subject has survived to $t_i$). The second step's $=$ is exact:
the $\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t$ factors cancel algebraically between
numerator and denominator, leaving a ratio that is independent of
$\Delta t$ altogether.

In the realized data, let $(i)$ denote the subject who actually
failed at $t_i$ (uniquely defined under the no-ties assumption).
Since the ratio $\theta(\vx_f)/\sum_{k \in R(t_i)}\theta(\vx_k)$
is already free of $\Delta t$, the $\Delta t \to 0$ limit is trivial;
substituting $f = (i)$ gives the partial-likelihood
contribution $\Lik^*_i(\b)$ from @def-ph-partial-lik:

$$
\lim_{\Delta t \to 0}
\Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails} \mid 1 \text{ failure at } t_i,\ R(t_i)}
\;=\;
\frac{\theta(\vx_{(i)})}{\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \theta(\vx_k)}
\;=\; \Lik^*_i(\b).
$$

The **partial likelihood** [@cox1972regression] takes the product of
these per-event conditional probabilities across the $D$ observed
failure times:

$$
\Lik^*(\b)
\;\eqdef\; \prod_{i=1}^{D} \Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails} \mid 1 \text{ failure at } t_i,\ R(t_i)}.
$$

Under the no-ties assumption, each ordered event time $t_i$ has
$d_i = 1$, so $\prod_{i=1}^{D}(\cdots) = \prod_{\{i:\,d_i=1\}}(\cdots)$,
and this matches the form in @def-ph-partial-lik.

To see exactly which part of the failure probability the partial
likelihood discards,
factor each event's contribution.
The chain rule used above
($A \subset B$, so $\Pr(A \mid C) = \Pr(A \mid B \cap C)\,\Pr(B \mid C)$)
splits the probability that subject $(i)$ fails at $t_i$
into a *which-subject-fails* factor and a *whether-a-failure-occurs* factor:

$$
\ba
\underbrace{\Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}}
          _{\approx\ \haz_0(t_i)\,\theta(\vx_{(i)})\,\Delta t}
&= \underbrace{\Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails} \mid 1 \text{ failure at } t_i,\ R(t_i)}}
             _{\to\ \Lik^*_i(\b)\ =\ \theta(\vx_{(i)}) / \sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \theta(\vx_k)}
\\
&\quad \times\
\underbrace{\Pr\sb{1 \text{ failure at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}}
          _{\approx\ \haz_0(t_i)\,\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \theta(\vx_k)\,\Delta t}.
\ea
$$

Taking the product over the $D$ event times factors the joint
probability of the realized failure sequence into the partial
likelihood and a discarded factor:

$$
\ba
\prod_{i=1}^{D} \Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}
&\approx
\underbrace{\prod_{i=1}^{D} \frac{\theta(\vx_{(i)})}{\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \theta(\vx_k)}}
          _{\Lik^*(\b)\ \text{(kept)}}
\\
&\quad \times\
\underbrace{\prod_{i=1}^{D} \haz_0(t_i)\paren{\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \theta(\vx_k)}\Delta t}
          _{\text{discarded}}.
\ea
$$

The partial likelihood keeps the first product and drops the second.
The discarded factor is the only place the baseline hazard
$\haz_0(t_i)$ appears — equivalently, $\haz_0(t_i)$ cancels from each
kept factor — which is why maximizing $\Lik^*(\b)$ alone frees the
estimator from $\haz_0(\cdot)$.
The discarded factor is **not** free of $\b$, however:
it still depends on $\b$ through the risk-set sums
$\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \theta(\vx_k)$.
Dropping it therefore sacrifices the information about $\b$ carried by
*when* (and within how large a risk set) failures occur,
retaining only the information in *which* subject fails at each event time.

This partial likelihood product is a *construction* rather than a derivation of the joint
probability: the factors are not formally independent (the risk set
$R(t_{i+1})$ depends on who failed and was censored before $t_{i+1}$),
but Cox showed that treating $\Lik^*(\b)$ as a likelihood — i.e.,
maximizing it as a function of $\b$ — yields estimators with the
usual large-sample properties (consistency, asymptotic normality,
the standard score and information identities;
see also [@klein2003survival, §8.3, Theoretical Note 1]).
Under noninformative censoring, each factor depends on the observed
history only through $R(t_i)$ and $\{\vx_j\}_{j \in R(t_i)}$, and
substituting the per-event limit gives exactly the
partial likelihood $\Lik^*(\b)$ of @def-ph-partial-lik.

Because $\haz_0(t_i)$ and $\Delta t$ cancel within each factor,
the partial likelihood depends on $\b$ alone
and not on the baseline hazard $\haz_0(\cdot)$.

:::
